text: Transesophageal echo reveals an ostium secundum ASD.
The anterior surface of the femoral artery and vein are exposed.
A purse-string suture is placed around the femoral artery and vein using a foraproline suture.
Circumferential dissection around the vessel is not necessary.
It is important to limit lymphatic disruption during the dissection.
The pleural cavity is carefully entered, and the pericardium is opened two centimeters from the phrenic nerve.
Pericardial stay sutures are then placed.
It's important to be strategic about the suture location and retraction.
Additional sutures are then placed at the lower boredom of the pericardium.
The SVC is then snared using vessel loops.
We believe elastic vessel loops provide a more hemostatic seal than umbilical tape.
The IVC is snared in a similar fashion.
We believe a vertical incision offers the best exposure since it provides visualization around the venous cannula.
The incision is extended upwards and then towards the right atrial appendage.
We have found that using a right angle scissors or POTS works best.
Once the atrium is open, the ASD is easily visualized.
We then close our septal defect using either bovine or autologous pericardium.
This is done using a 5-0 proline suture in a running fashion.
When using either bovine or autologous pericardium, it's important that the smooth surface faces the left atrial side.
During closure, it's often helpful to have a pump sucker inside the left atrium to aid visibility.
Our approach is to start at the top of the septum defect and suture forehand on the left side.
When the bottom is reached, we start again at the top and suture forehand on the right side.
While closing any right-sided lesion, it's important to identify the SVC, IVC, tricuspid valve, and coronary sinus to avoid inadvertent injury.
The final septal closure can be seen here.
The right atrium is enclosed in the standard fashion using a 4-0 proline suture.
We prefer a two-layer closure.
Lastly, we believe cryoablation of the intercostal nerves and injection of Expirel provides excellent postoperative pain control.
We prefer to freeze two inner spaces below and two inner spaces above the incision.
This final four-chamber TEE shows no communication between the atrial chambers.
